scenarios.title: Tabletop Exercise: Ransomware on Registration Day

briefing.subject: Facilitate a ransomware tabletop exercise
briefing.body: On the first day of fall registration, when about 9,000 students try to enroll in a single morning, ransomware encrypts file servers and the Banner registration front end. You will design and facilitate a tabletop exercise around this exact day, following NIST SP 800-84. The materials below give you a timed inject schedule, the roles who must be in the room, and the known gaps in our current incident response plan. Drive the discussion inject by inject, make each role own a decision, and capture every gap in an after-action report. The hardest tension you must surface is the pressure to restore registration fast for 38,000 students against the need to preserve forensic evidence and avoid reencrypting clean systems. Work only from the materials, and defend your facilitation choices and your gap findings against them.
objectives: Design a realistic scenario with escalating injects tied to the real plan
Facilitate decisions across the right roles under time pressure
Capture every gap and owned action item in an after-action report

dossier.title: Inject schedule
dossier.intro: The timed injects you will deliver during the exercise. Each inject must force a decision from a named role.

rows: Help desk reports staff cannot open shared drives; ransom note appears on three servers
Declare an incident or keep triaging? Who has authority to declare?
Banner registration page returns errors; 9,000 students are mid-enrollment
Take registration offline to contain, or keep it up to serve students?
EDR shows the same encryptor process on 14 more hosts spreading by SMB
Isolate the network segment now, or wait to map the full blast radius?
Attacker portal claims 60 GB of student records were copied before encryption
Is this a reportable breach? Who decides notification and on what clock?
Backups exist but were last test-restored 11 months ago; restore would overwrite forensic images
Restore fast for students, or hold to preserve evidence and confirm eradication?

dossier.title: Roles in the room
bullets: Incident commander (CISO): declares the incident and owns the response decisions.
SOC lead: runs containment, isolation, and EDR actions.
Registrar and IT operations: own Banner and the registration service availability.
GRC and legal counsel: own breach determination and the notification clock.
Communications and the President's chief of staff: own messaging to students, faculty, and the cabinet.
Backup and recovery engineer: owns restore decisions and evidence preservation.

dossier.title: Known plan gaps
bullets: The current IR plan names no single person with authority to take Banner offline. It defaults to a committee, which stalls under time pressure.
Breach notification roles are undefined. The plan says report promptly but names no decision owner and no legal clock.
Backups are not test-restored on a schedule. The last successful test restore was 11 months ago.
There is no written rule on preserving forensic images before a restore, so a fast restore could destroy the only evidence.
No pre-drafted student or cabinet communications exist, so messaging would be written live during the crisis.

deliverables: Tabletop plan and inject deck (Word document)
The scenario narrative, the timed injects from the schedule, and the specific decision each role must make at each inject.
After-action report (Word document)
Following NIST SP 800-84: what was decided at each inject, every gap that surfaced, and the corrective action items with named owners and due dates.
In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership: the top three gaps the exercise exposed and the single fix to fund first.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present your after-action findings to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: Per NIST SP 800-84, what makes this tabletop worth running?
options: It tests the real IR plan and the real decision-makers against a believable registration-day scenario.
It scores each participant so HR can rank performance.
It uses a scenario so extreme no plan could ever cover it, to humble the team.

steps.prompt: The plan names no owner for taking Banner offline. How should you use that in the exercise?
options: Assign yourself the authority so the exercise keeps moving.
Skip the 08:25 inject so the team is not embarrassed.
Deliver the 08:25 inject and let the missing authority surface as a gap to record.

steps.prompt: At 09:10 EDR shows the encryptor spreading by SMB to 14 more hosts. What is the best facilitation move?
options: End the exercise because containment is now obvious.
Press the SOC lead to choose: isolate the segment now or keep mapping, and record why.
Tell the team the answer so you stay on schedule.

steps.prompt: The 10:30 inject says 60 GB of student records were copied. Who should own the notification decision in the room?
options: GRC and legal counsel, working the breach determination and the legal clock.
The help desk, since they saw it first.
Nobody decides during a tabletop; it is just talk.

steps.prompt: The 12:00 inject sets up the central tension. What should the exercise force the team to confront?
options: How to phrase the apology email.
Which vendor to blame in the press release.
Whether to restore fast for 38,000 students versus preserve forensic images and confirm eradication first.

steps.prompt: What is the real output of an 800-84 tabletop?
options: A pass or fail grade for the team.
An after-action report listing gaps with corrective actions, owners, and due dates.
A verbal agreement that things went fine.

questions.prompt: From the plan gaps, which gap does the 08:25 registration inject most directly expose?
options: No single person has authority to take Banner offline.
Backups are not tested on a schedule.
No pre-drafted student communications exist.
Forensic image preservation is undefined.

questions.prompt: The 10:30 data-theft inject is designed to surface which two gaps? Select two.
options: Undefined breach notification roles and clock.
No pre-drafted cabinet and student messaging.
Untested backups.
Missing network segmentation.

questions.prompt: Why is the 12:00 backup inject the hardest one to facilitate?
options: Because backups never matter in ransomware.
Because the registrar is not in the room.
Because it forces the restore-fast versus preserve-evidence tension with no written rule to settle it.
Because the encryptor has already been removed.

questions.prompt: Using the roles table, who should make the call at 08:05 to formally declare the incident?
options: The backup and recovery engineer.
The communications lead.
The registrar.
The incident commander, the CISO.

hints: Each inject should map to a known plan gap and force a named role to decide.
The data-theft inject is where breach notification roles and the legal clock have to come up.
The backup inject is the real test: restoring fast for students fights preserving evidence and confirming eradication.

scenarios.title: Cyber Insurance Readiness Assessment

briefing.subject: Cyber insurance renewal readiness
briefing.body: Our cyber liability policy renews on October 1, and the carrier's renewal application asks pointed yes-or-no questions about our security controls. This matters more than it looks: if we attest yes to a control we do not actually have and later file a claim, the carrier can rescind the policy and deny payment when we need it most. The materials below are the questionnaire, with each control the insurer asks about, our honest current state, and the answer that current state supports. Several honest answers are no. Work through every row, map the gaps, and prioritize the fixes that most improve coverage and reduce premium risk before the renewal date. Use the materials and be ready to defend why an answer is yes or no.
objectives: Map each insurer control question to our honest current state
Identify exactly where the truthful answer is no
Prioritize the fixes that most improve coverage before October 1

dossier.title: Renewal application questionnaire
dossier.intro: Each control the carrier asks about, our honest current state, and the answer that state supports. Renewal date is October 1.

rows: MFA on all email and remote access
Enforced for IT staff and remote VPN, but not on faculty or staff email
MFA on all privileged and admin accounts
Enforced on all domain admin and Banner admin accounts
EDR deployed on all endpoints and servers
EDR on 94% of endpoints; 140 servers and lab machines uncovered
Backups are tested by restore at least annually
Backups run nightly to immutable storage but have never been test-restored
Proofpoint gateway in place, blocking about 1,200 phishing emails per week
Written incident response plan tested in the last 12 months
A plan exists from three years ago and has never been exercised
Privileged access management and least privilege reviewed
No PAM tool; access recertification last ran 19 months ago

dossier.title: How carriers weight these controls
bullets: MFA coverage is the single strongest factor carriers use to price and even to offer a policy. A no here can drive a surcharge or a declination.
Tested, immutable backups are weighted heavily because they directly reduce ransomware loss. Untested backups do not count as tested.
EDR coverage gaps matter most on servers, which is where the uncovered 140 machines sit.
A current, exercised IR plan signals the org can limit a loss. A three-year-old untested plan does not meet the question.
Material misrepresentation on the application is grounds for the carrier to rescind coverage after a claim.

dossier.title: Constraints before renewal
paras: MFA on faculty and staff email can be enabled inside the existing license at no new cost; the work is rollout and support, about three weeks.
A first full backup test restore can be scheduled within two weeks and is the cheapest control to flip from no to yes.
Full EDR coverage on the 140 servers needs procurement and is a longer effort. A PAM deployment is the longest and most expensive of all the gaps.

deliverables: Readiness gap analysis (Word or Excel)
One row per questionnaire item: the honest current state, the supported yes-or-no answer, the gap, and the fix with an owner and effort.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for the CFO: where coverage is at risk, and the two or three fixes to prioritize before October 1 ranked by coverage benefit per effort.
In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Attestation worksheet (Word or Excel)
The exact answers you would submit on the application, with a defensible note for every no.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the readiness gaps and your prioritized fixes to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: Why does it matter that every application answer is accurate?
options: Carriers never verify the answers, so it is a formality.
Accurate answers always lower the premium.
A material misrepresentation lets the carrier rescind the policy and deny a future claim.

steps.prompt: From the questionnaire, the MFA-on-email row should be answered:
options: No, because faculty and staff email is not covered.
Yes, since privileged accounts already have MFA.
Skip it, because email is low risk.

steps.prompt: Backups run nightly to immutable storage but have never been restore-tested. The truthful answer to the tested-backups question is:
options: Yes, the backups exist and are immutable.
No, until a restore is actually tested.
Not applicable.

steps.prompt: Given the constraints, which fix flips a no to a strong yes fastest and cheapest?
options: Run the first full backup test restore, schedulable within two weeks at no procurement cost.
Buy EDR for the 140 uncovered servers.
Deploy a PAM tool across all privileged accounts.

steps.prompt: What does the CFO most need from your brief?
options: The IT organization chart.
Where coverage is at risk and the prioritized fixes to make before October 1.
A list of every vendor the college uses.

questions.prompt: From the questionnaire, how many controls honestly answer no?
options: Two.
Seven.
Five.
None.

questions.prompt: Which two fixes give the most coverage benefit for the least effort before renewal? Select two.
options: Enable MFA on faculty and staff email within the existing license.
Run the first backup test restore.
Deploy a PAM tool.
Replace the email gateway.

questions.prompt: Why is reporting backups as tested when they were never restored dangerous?
options: It raises the premium.
It has no effect on coverage.
It violates HIPAA directly.
It is a material misrepresentation that can let the carrier deny a ransomware claim.

questions.prompt: Which questionnaire row is an honest yes despite the MFA-on-email row being a no?
options: EDR deployed on all endpoints and servers.
Written incident response plan tested in the last 12 months.
MFA on all privileged and admin accounts.
Privileged access management reviewed.

hints: Answer every row from the honest current state column, not from what sounds good.
MFA coverage is the control carriers weight most heavily, so a no there is the costliest gap.
The cheapest no to flip is the untested backup. A first test restore is fast and free.

scenarios.title: Asset Inventory and Shadow-IT Review

briefing.subject: Asset inventory and shadow IT across departments
briefing.body: Departments across the college keep buying their own apps and devices on purchasing cards without telling IT, and you cannot protect what you do not know exists. The materials below give you our inventory-completeness numbers and a discovered shadow-IT list, including tools that hold student data outside any security review. Work the numbers, judge how trustworthy the inventory is, and rank the shadow IT by the sensitivity of the data each tool holds. Then recommend a lightweight intake process so new assets and apps get registered and assessed before they go live, anchored in NIST SP 800-53 CM controls. Use only the materials and defend your risk ranking and your process design against them.
objectives: Assess how complete and current the asset inventory really is
Rank the discovered shadow IT by data sensitivity and risk
Recommend a lightweight intake process to register and assess new assets

dossier.title: Inventory completeness
dossier.intro: How much of the environment the official CMDB actually accounts for, by category.

rows: Network and IoT devices (cameras, printers, lab gear)

dossier.title: Discovered shadow IT
dossier.intro: Apps found through expense-report and DNS review that IT never approved or assessed.

rows: Student names, IDs, and disability accommodations
Aid applications with SSNs and tax data
Student essays and graded work pasted in

dossier.title: Why this is the blind spot
bullets: The departmental SaaS and IoT categories are under half covered, so the riskiest, fastest-growing parts of the environment are the least known.
Three shadow-IT tools hold regulated data: accommodation records and aid data are FERPA and GLBA covered, and the SSNs in the file share fall under state breach law.
None of the four unsanctioned tools went through a security review, a data processing agreement, or a vendor risk assessment.
Free-tier AI tools often train on submitted content, so pasting student work in can leak FERPA-protected records.
There is no intake process today, so buying on a purchasing card is faster and easier than asking IT.

deliverables: Inventory gap and shadow-IT findings (Word or Excel)
The coverage gaps by category, the discovered shadow IT ranked by data sensitivity, the risk each poses, and a recommended owner per item.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership: the riskiest unmanaged assets and the proposed intake process, with the single tool to address first.
In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Intake process design (Word document)
A simple register-and-assess workflow for new apps and devices, mapped to NIST SP 800-53 CM controls.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the inventory gaps and your intake process to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: Why is asset inventory the foundation for the rest of security?
options: It is only needed for accounting and depreciation.
You cannot patch, monitor, or defend an asset you do not know exists.
It matters only for physical devices, not software.

steps.prompt: From the completeness table, which gap should worry you most?
options: Managed workstations at 96% coverage.
Servers at 90% coverage.
Departmental SaaS at 41% coverage, where data-holding apps hide.

steps.prompt: Which shadow-IT tool is the highest risk to address first?
options: The personal cloud file share in Financial Aid holding SSNs and tax data.
The approved LMS plugin in Online Learning.
The consumer scheduling app holding class schedules.

steps.prompt: What most reduces shadow IT going forward, per the CM control family?
options: Ban all departmental purchasing of software forever.
Ignore it, since departments know their own needs.
A simple, fast intake process so new apps and devices get registered and assessed before going live.

steps.prompt: What belongs in the leadership brief?
options: The inventory gaps, the shadow-IT risks ranked by data sensitivity, and the proposed intake process.
A raw device count and nothing else.
An apology with no recommendations.

questions.prompt: Which asset category has the weakest inventory coverage?
options: Managed workstations.
Servers and VMs.
Departmental SaaS applications.
None; all are above 90%.

questions.prompt: Which two shadow-IT tools hold regulated data that pulls in FERPA or GLBA? Select two.
options: The survey tool holding disability accommodation records.
The personal cloud file share holding aid applications with SSNs.
The approved LMS plugin.
The consumer scheduling app holding class schedules.

questions.prompt: Why is the free-tier AI tutoring chatbot a FERPA concern specifically?
options: It costs money the department did not budget.
Free AI tools may train on submitted content, leaking pasted student work and grades.
It is slower than the approved tools.
It only works on personal devices.

questions.prompt: Combining the two estimated categories, roughly how many SaaS and IoT assets are missing from the CMDB?
options: About 50.
About 100.
About 250.
About 920.

hints: Read the coverage column. The lowest number is where the real risk hides.
Rank shadow IT by the sensitivity of the data each tool holds, not by department size.
Make the right way the easy way. A simple intake process beats both a ban and a blind eye.

scenarios.title: Controls Assessment and Audit: SIS

briefing.subject: Controls assessment of the Banner SIS
briefing.body: Internal Audit needs an independent assessment of the security controls protecting Banner, the Student Information System for 38,000 students. Using NIST SP 800-53A as the method, you will test each control two ways: is it implemented, and is it actually operating effectively. The materials below are our control-test results, with the evidence gathered for each. Several controls exist on paper but are not followed in practice, and your job is to call those out as findings. Document each result, attach the evidence, and write a recommendation with an owner and a due date for every gap. Work from the materials and defend each finding against the evidence, not against opinion.
objectives: Confirm the controls in scope and the 800-53A assessment method
Test each control for both implementation and effectiveness
Document findings with evidence and owned, dated recommendations

dossier.title: Control test results
dossier.intro: Each control assessed for implementation and effectiveness, with the objective evidence gathered.

rows: Policy requires quarterly review; last review was 19 months ago; 230 separated-employee accounts still active
Enforced for IT staff only; 0% of student and 59% of faculty accounts lack MFA
Logs are collected but no one reviews them; no documented review since the SIEM was installed
Nightly immutable backups confirmed; last successful test restore was 8 months ago, outside the annual requirement
Hardened baseline applied and verified on all Banner servers in the last scan
27 standard users hold registrar admin rights with no documented business need

dossier.title: The 800-53A method
bullets: Each control objective is assessed by three methods: examine the artifacts, interview the staff, and test the control in operation.
Implemented means the control exists and is in place. Effective means it is operating as intended and producing the result it should.
A control can be implemented but not effective. A documented policy that nobody follows is the classic example, and it is still a finding.
Findings are rated by the risk of the gap, and each needs a recommendation, a named owner, and a due date to drive remediation.
Independence is what gives the assessment credibility with leadership and the audit committee. The assessor must not own the controls being tested.

dossier.title: Audit committee context
paras: The committee meets quarterly and expects findings ranked by risk, not a flat list. The two account-related findings, AC-2 and AC-6, both leave excessive standing access into a 38,000-student system and rate highest.
One control, CM-6, passed both implementation and effectiveness and should be reported as a strength, not buried among the gaps. Reporting what works is part of a credible assessment.

deliverables: Controls assessment report (Word document)
For each control: the test method, the evidence, the implemented and effective results, the finding if any, and a recommendation with owner and due date.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for the audit committee on overall control effectiveness, the highest-risk findings, and the one strength to acknowledge.
In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Findings and remediation tracker (Word or Excel)
Every finding ranked by risk, with owner, due date, and status, ready for the next committee meeting.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the control findings and recommendations to leadership as if briefing the audit committee. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: What does an 800-53A assessment actually determine?
options: How many users the system has.
Whether each control is implemented and operating effectively.
The dollar value of the server hardware.

steps.prompt: Per the method, how is a control objectively tested?
options: By examining artifacts, interviewing staff, and testing the control in operation.
By assuming it works because a policy exists.
By asking the control owner once and moving on.

steps.prompt: AC-2 has a quarterly-review policy but the last review was 19 months ago and 230 separated accounts are active. The result is:
options: Fully effective, since the policy exists.
Out of scope for the assessment.
Implemented but not effective, a finding.

steps.prompt: Which finding should rate highest for the committee?
options: CP-9 backups, where the test restore is 8 months old.
AC-6 least privilege, with 27 users holding registrar admin rights with no business need.
CM-6 configuration baseline, which passed.

steps.prompt: What must every finding carry to drive remediation?
options: A recommendation, a named owner, and a due date.
Just a description of what is wrong.
A note that it will be looked at eventually.

steps.prompt: Why does the assessor's independence matter to this audit?
options: It does not; only the findings matter.
It exists only to slow the process down.
An independent assessor gives leadership and the committee a credible, unbiased result.

questions.prompt: From the results table, how many controls are findings (implemented but not fully effective)?
options: One.
Three.
Five.
All six.

questions.prompt: Which two findings leave excessive standing access into the SIS and rate highest? Select two.
options: AC-2 with 230 active separated-employee accounts.
AC-6 with 27 users holding unneeded registrar admin rights.
CM-6 configuration baseline.
CP-9 backup test restore timing.

questions.prompt: Why should CM-6 appear in the report even though it is not a finding?
options: To pad the report length.
Because reporting a control that works is part of a credible, balanced assessment.
Because it is the highest risk.
It should not appear at all.

questions.prompt: The IA-2 evidence shows MFA enforced for IT staff only. What is the most defensible result for that control?
options: Not implemented at all.
Implemented and fully effective.
Out of scope.
Partially implemented and not effective, a finding.

hints: 800-53A asks whether a control works, not just whether it is written down.
Gather objective evidence three ways: examine, interview, test.
Rank findings by risk. Excessive standing access into a 38,000-student system outranks an overdue backup test.

scenarios.title: Acceptable Use Policy Development

briefing.subject: Refresh the acceptable use policy
briefing.body: Our acceptable use policy was last updated in 2014 and predates the cloud, the smartphone era at work, and generative AI entirely. Two recent incidents traced straight back to the gaps, and the policy gives us no clear rule to point to. The materials below are a gap analysis of the current AUP against the topics a modern policy must cover, plus the two incidents that exposed the holes. Work the gap table, judge which gaps matter most, and draft a refreshed policy that is plain-language, specific, and actually enforceable. Then plan the review, approval, and acknowledgment so it can be adopted. Use the materials and defend your priorities and your wording choices against them.
objectives: Identify what a modern AUP must cover from the gap analysis
Write clear, specific, enforceable rules in plain language
Plan review, approval, and a tracked acknowledgment for adoption

dossier.title: AUP gap analysis
dossier.intro: Each topic a modern AUP should cover, whether the current 2014 policy covers it, and the issue.

rows: No rule on storing college data in personal Dropbox, Drive, or similar
Mentions personal laptops once but sets no security or data rules for phones
Silent on pasting student data or college data into AI tools
Covered, but references a retired email system and an old reporting address
Still references peer-to-peer file sharing and instant messenger, not modern apps
States rules exist but names no consequences and no acknowledgment requirement

dossier.title: Incidents that exposed the gaps
bullets: A staff member uploaded a spreadsheet of 1,400 student records to a personal cloud drive to work from home. No AUP rule prohibited it, so there was nothing to enforce.
A faculty member pasted a roster of student names and grades into a free AI chatbot to draft feedback. The policy said nothing about AI, and the data may now be retained by the vendor.
In both cases, HR asked which policy was violated and the answer was none clearly enough to act on.

dossier.title: What makes an AUP enforceable
paras: Rules people cannot understand do not get followed, and rules that are vague cannot be enforced. Plain language and specific prohibitions are what make a policy usable.
An AUP touches many groups, so it needs review by legal, HR, IT, and student representatives before approval. Skipping a group invites a rule that cannot be applied to them.
Enforcement rests on two things: formal leadership approval and a recorded acknowledgment from each user. Without the acknowledgment, you cannot show the user agreed to the rule you are enforcing.

deliverables: Draft acceptable use policy (Word document)
The full refreshed policy: scope, plain-language rules covering cloud, BYOD, and AI, consequences, and an acknowledgment section.
Executive brief (Word document)
One page for leadership on what changed since 2014, which gaps the recent incidents exposed, and how the policy will be adopted.
In-app assessment
Work the assessment steps and the graded questions on the Assessment Workflow screen. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Adoption plan (Word document)
The review, approval, and tracked-acknowledgment steps, with the groups to involve and the rollout communication.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present the new policy and adoption plan to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: From the gap table, which topics is the 2014 AUP completely silent on?
options: Email and acceptable personal use.
Enforcement and consequences only.
Cloud and personal file-sharing and generative AI tools.

steps.prompt: How should the new rules be written so people follow and you can enforce them?
options: In dense legalese that covers every edge case.
In plain language with specific, enforceable prohibitions.
As vague suggestions to allow flexibility.

steps.prompt: The faculty AI incident shows the AUP needs which specific rule?
options: A clear rule against pasting student or college data into AI tools without approval.
A ban on all email attachments.
A rule about peer-to-peer file sharing.

steps.prompt: Who should review the draft before approval?
options: Only the policy author.
No one; speed matters more.
Legal, HR, IT, and student representatives.

steps.prompt: What two things make the policy enforceable?
options: Formal leadership approval and a recorded acknowledgment from each user.
Posting it on a website and hoping people read it.
Word of mouth from supervisors.

steps.prompt: How should the refreshed AUP be rolled out?
options: Posted once with no announcement.
Kept internal to IT.
Communicated to students and staff with a tracked acknowledgment.

questions.prompt: From the gap table, which two topics is the current AUP completely missing? Select two.
options: Cloud and personal file-sharing services.
Generative AI tools.
Email and phishing responsibilities.
Acceptable personal use.

questions.prompt: What did both recent incidents have in common?
options: Malware was installed on a server.
There was no clear AUP rule for HR to enforce against.
An outside attacker breached the network.
The email gateway failed.

questions.prompt: What two things make the refreshed policy enforceable?
options: A long appendix and a glossary.
Broad, general language that tries to cover everything.
Posting it on the intranet only.
Formal leadership approval and a recorded user acknowledgment.

questions.prompt: The staff cloud-upload incident most directly argues for adding which rule to the AUP?
options: A prohibition on storing college data in personal cloud or file-sharing services.
A rule requiring quarterly password changes.
A ban on personal laptops entirely.
A rule about acceptable break-room use.

hints: Start with the topics marked not covered: cloud and AI are the new gaps.
Both incidents failed for the same reason, no clear rule to enforce. Write that rule plainly.
Enforceability needs two things: approval and a recorded acknowledgment from each user.

scenarios.title: Vendor Breach Response: Billing SaaS

briefing.subject: Breach notice from the BVHN billing vendor
briefing.body: MedLedger, the SaaS billing vendor for our BVHN health partner, sent a breach notice at 6:40 this morning. Their integration uses one OAuth token granted into our cloud tenant, and that token can read the BVHN billing dataset, about 41,200 patient billing records. MedLedger says an attacker stole a set of customer API tokens, but they will not confirm whether ours was among them. Do not take their word for it. Confirm in the SIEM feed whether the token was actually used to pull data from our side and where it went, then use the materials below to reassess the vendor with HECVAT and check the business associate agreement. The data is patient billing, so HIPAA and the BAA are both in scope, and the notification clock may already be running.
objectives: Confirm whether the vendor token accessed our data and what was taken
Scope the exposure and the regulated data involved
Reassess the vendor's risk and verify the business associate agreement

iocs.note: Destination the vendor token data was pushed to

dossier.title: MedLedger vendor profile and prior rating
dossier.intro: What we knew about this vendor before the breach.

rows: Cloud billing and claims for BVHN
Yes, signed 2 years ago, never reviewed since
Single OAuth token, read scope on billing dataset

dossier.title: What the token could reach
dossier.intro: Scope of the OAuth grant and the data at stake.

dossier.title: HECVAT and BAA context
bullets: The signed BAA requires MedLedger to report a security incident to us within 5 business days. Their notice arrived 11 days after their stated detection date.
The prior HECVAT did not require token rotation, encryption-at-rest attestation, or proof of a SOC 2 report. Those gaps were accepted as conditions to revisit at renewal.
HIPAA gives a covered entity up to 60 days to notify affected individuals once a breach is known. The breach is known to us as of today.
MedLedger holds PHI for BVHN, which makes them a business associate. A current BAA with breach-notification terms is mandatory under HIPAA.

deliverables: In-app investigation and assessment
Confirm the exposure in the logs, work the assessment steps, and answer the graded questions. This produces your Blackboard completion code.
Vendor risk reassessment (HECVAT) (Word document)

Re-rate MedLedger's risk in light of the breach, using the HECVAT questionnaire as your framework. Cite the late notice, the never-rotated token, and the missing attestations, then recommend approve with conditions, suspend, or terminate.
Business associate agreement review (Word document)
Confirm the BAA is in place and check its breach-notification and data-handling terms against what actually happened, including the 5-day reporting clause the vendor appears to have missed.
Executive brief and notification memo (Word document)
One page for leadership and a draft notification decision: who must be told, by when, under HIPAA and state breach law.
Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes

Required. On camera, present what the token accessed, the regulated data involved, and your vendor recommendation to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

steps.prompt: What in the feed confirms the vendor token actually pulled data, not just that a breach was reported?

scenarios.title: FERPA Data Exposure: Misconfigured Cloud Share

briefing.subject: Public cloud share holding student records
briefing.body: The Advising department set a cloud folder of student records to public so an off-site staffer could read it, and search engines began indexing it. We found it when a student googled their own name and saw a transcript link. The folder was open for about 9 days before we caught it. Confirm in the SIEM feed what was exposed and whether anyone actually pulled the data, then use the records inventory below to scope the FERPA exposure and determine the notification duty. Contain the exposure first, before you write a single word of analysis.
objectives: Confirm the exposure and whether records were downloaded
Scope the FERPA records involved
Determine the breach-notification duty and recommend notification

iocs.note: Destination records were copied to after public exposure

dossier.intro: What sat in the public folder, by type and sensitivity.

dossier.title: Exposure window and indexing evidence
bullets: The folder permission changed from private to anyone-with-the-link, then to fully public, on a date 9 days before discovery.
Search-engine cache shows at least 3 transcript files were indexed and appeared in public results.
Cloud access logs show downloads from one external address that is not on any known college or partner network.
There is no DLP rule today that alerts on a folder of student records being made public.

dossier.title: Notification context
paras: FERPA does not impose a fixed breach-notification deadline, but it requires the institution to address unauthorized disclosure of education records and may require notice as a matter of policy and best practice. The 210 SSNs pull in Texas breach-notification law, which does set deadlines and may require notice to the state attorney general above a threshold of affected residents. Scope first, because the count of affected students and the data types decide which notices are mandatory versus discretionary.

scenarios.title: Financial-Aid Phishing to GLBA Incident

briefing.subject: Compromised financial aid mailbox
briefing.body: A financial aid officer clicked a phishing link and entered credentials on a fake login page yesterday afternoon. The account now has a hidden inbox rule that auto-deletes any message containing the words wire, payment, or invoice, and a fraudulent wire request for 48,500 dollars went out to a new vendor account this morning. Aid data is GLBA-covered, so this is both a security incident and a compliance event. Confirm the compromise in the SIEM feed, use the materials to scope the GLBA data the mailbox touched, then handle containment and the GLBA incident report and notification analysis.

scenarios.title: Stolen Laptop with PII: Risk Determination

briefing.subject: Stolen staff laptop with student PII
briefing.body: An advisor's laptop was stolen from a parked car two nights ago. It held a local spreadsheet with about 120 student records, including names, IDs, and some grades. The asset record shows BitLocker full-disk encryption was enabled and the device was powered off at the time. Since then we see a handful of failed login attempts from an unfamiliar address against the advisor's account, but no confirmed successful logon. The core task is a breach risk determination, not a panic. Confirm the post-theft attempts in the SIEM feed, then use the device and encryption materials below to decide whether the PII was actually at risk and whether that triggers a notification. Do not default to mandatory notice without the analysis.
objectives: Confirm post-theft access attempts in the logs
Determine whether the PII was actually at risk, given encryption
Make a documented breach risk determination and notification decision

iocs.note: Unfamiliar address attempting access after the theft

dossier.title: Device and data record
dossier.intro: The asset record for the stolen laptop and what it held.

rows: BitLocker enabled and confirmed in MDM
Issued, device offline, not yet confirmed

dossier.title: Post-theft access-attempt summary
dossier.intro: What the logs show against the advisor's account after the theft.

rows: Failed login attempts from unfamiliar IP

dossier.title: Encryption safe-harbor context
paras: Most state breach-notification laws, including Texas, provide a safe harbor when the lost data was encrypted and the decryption key was not also compromised. The device was powered off, so the BitLocker keys were not resident in memory and the disk is at rest and unreadable without the recovery key. The failed logins are against the cloud account, not the disk, and none succeeded. The determination must weigh encryption status, evidence of access, and data sensitivity together, and it must be documented even when the outcome is low risk. A documented low-risk determination is defensible. Concealing a loss is not.

deliverables: In-app investigation and assessment
Confirm the access attempts in the logs, work the assessment steps, and answer the graded questions. This produces your Blackboard completion code.

Executive brief and notification decision (Word document)

Recorded presentation video, 5 to 8 minutes
Required. On camera, present your risk determination and notification decision to leadership as if briefing the cabinet. Attach the recording file to your Blackboard submission.

options: Requiring staff to never take laptops off campus

options: A documented low-risk determination or safe harbor

scenarios.title: Ransomware on the Registrar: BIA-Driven Recovery

briefing.subject: Ransomware in the registrar's office
briefing.body: Ransomware hit the registrar's office overnight, mid-semester, with grade submission deadlines this week. It moved in from a compromised host, disabled endpoint defenses, encrypted file shares and the registration database, and dropped ransom notes demanding 1.2 million dollars in cryptocurrency. Confirm the scope and entry path in the SIEM feed, then drive the recovery from the governance side. Use the business impact analysis below to decide what comes back first, confirm the backups are clean and offline, and brief leadership on the recovery plan and a documented decision on the ransom. We have immutable backups, so favor recovery over payment, but make it a reasoned call.

Clean immutable and offline backups exist with a recovery point of about 6 hours, so most data is recoverable without paying.
Paying does not guarantee a working decryptor, may violate sanctions rules depending on the actor, and funds future attacks.
The ransom decision is a governance and legal matter, made with leadership and counsel and documented, not decided by one administrator.
